title: The cut
summary: A documentary filmmaker and one of her subjects argue, two years after the film's release, over a sequence the subject now wants removed.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: Thank you for coming, Marcus. I wanted to have this in person.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: I appreciate that. But my decision is not really up for discussion. I'd like the diner sequence removed. Everything else, you can keep.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: I want to honour that. But also to understand it. Two years ago you watched a rough cut of that scene, in this same room, and said — your words — 'leave it in, that's the whole point.' What's changed?

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: I've changed. Or rather, I've learned what it means to be on the other side of a camera that does not blink. The scene is accurate. Every word I said, I said. But the way the silence sits after my mother walks out tells a story about me that I no longer think is the only one.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: I take that seriously. But I want to push back on one piece. The frame did not invent the silence. The silence happened. My job was to point a camera at it and not flinch.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: I'm not accusing you of fabricating anything. I'm saying selecting is itself a kind of writing. You chose those eight seconds out of three hours — no music, no cut, no relief. That's not 'pointing a camera.' That's a sentence you wrote, in my face.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: Fair. Editing is authorship. I won't pretend otherwise. But those choices are doing exactly what documentary is supposed to do. They refuse to soften something the world hands us hard.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: And the world that watches the film? My nephew, my students, the man in the diner who served us — they've all seen it. I don't control what eight seconds mean to them. You sat with the footage for nine months and arrived at a meaning. They get it in eight seconds and arrive at a verdict.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: I understand. But you signed a release. Not just the legal form — you watched the cut and said yes. If I let subjects withdraw consent two years after release, every documentary I have ever made becomes provisional. The form falls apart.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: I knew you'd say that. And I think you're partly right. But consider what you're defending. The consent I gave was the consent of a man who had not yet been recognised on a train by a stranger using my mother's first name as a punchline. The 'me' who said yes did not know what yes would cost.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: That is a real point, and I won't dismiss it. But it cuts both ways. The 'me' who agreed to make the film also did not know what it would do, and committed in advance to standing behind it. If consent only survives until it becomes inconvenient, it isn't consent — it's preference.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: You're treating my request as if it were a whim. It isn't. I'm asking you to remove one scene, not the film. The neighbourhood, my mother, the closing of the diner — all that can stay. What I'm asking is that the eight seconds, lifted from the worst minute of my life, not be the still that lives on the festival poster.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: That's a different request, and fairer. The poster I can change. The trailer I can re-cut. Whether I should remove the scene from the film itself — I don't know.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: The film is ninety-three minutes. It can be ninety-two.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: But the scene isn't decoration. It's the hinge. The second half of the film is calibrated against that silence. Without it, the ending stops meaning what it means. You called it 'the whole point' two years ago because it was.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: It was the whole point of the film you wanted to make. I'm not sure it was the whole point of my life.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: That's the cleanest sentence you've said all afternoon. What if we did this: existing prints stay as they are. For future distribution — streaming, broadcast — we offer a shorter cut with the scene removed, and your introduction at the beginning. The original stays in the archive. The version most people see going forward is the one you can stand behind.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: An author's edit, but from the subject.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: A subject's edit. The form might need to grow one.

body.speaker: Marcus
body.text: Let me think. I came here ready to fight you, and instead you've offered something I didn't know I could ask for. I'll write next week.

body.speaker: Aiyana
body.text: That's enough for today. Whichever way you decide — the conversation we just had is one I should have had before the film came out, not after. That part is on me.

annotations.meaningVi: bản dựng thô (chưa hoàn thiện)

annotations.phrase: selecting is itself a kind of writing
annotations.meaningVi: việc chọn lựa bản thân nó đã là một dạng viết/sáng tác
tạm thời, có điều kiện, có thể bị rút lại
như một câu chốt gây cười (thường mang tính chế giễu)
có hai mặt (lập luận áp dụng cho cả hai bên)
bản lề; mấu chốt giữ mọi thứ xoay quanh nó
bản chỉnh sửa của tác giả (so với bản gốc đã phát hành)
không thực sự mở ra để thảo luận; ý đã quyết, không phải đang mời tranh luận
tôn trọng và thực thi điều đó (dùng 'honour' ở văn phong trang trọng thay vì 'respect')
phản biện, bác lại (một luận điểm cụ thể); ngôn ngữ tranh luận học thuật/chuyên nghiệp
không nhụt chí, không né tránh (đối mặt với thực tế khắc nghiệt mà không rút lui)
bịa đặt, dựng lên (sự thật giả mạo); từ vựng trang trọng hàm ý cáo buộc nghiêm trọng
từ chối làm mềm hoá đi; kiên quyết giữ nguyên sự khắc nghiệt của thực tế
rút lại sự đồng ý (thuật ngữ pháp lý/đạo đức học); hành động hủy bỏ sự cho phép đã cấp trước

annotations.phrase: committed in advance to standing behind it
annotations.meaningVi: đã cam kết từ trước rằng sẽ đứng sau và ủng hộ tác phẩm; nghĩa vụ tiên liệu
sự bốc đồng, ý thích nhất thời (hàm ý thiếu lý trí; dùng để hạ thấp yêu cầu của đối phương)
được lấy ra từ, trích xuất từ (hàm ý có chủ đích chọn lọc từ một ngữ cảnh lớn hơn)
được hiệu chỉnh, định chuẩn đối với (một yếu tố tham chiếu); từ vựng kỹ thuật/học thuật cao cấp
đã đi đến một ý nghĩa (sau quá trình suy ngẫm dài); khác với 'found a meaning' — nhấn mạnh hành trình tư duy
đi đến một phán quyết tức thời; ẩn dụ pháp đình — khán giả xét xử mà không có đủ bối cảnh
hình thức/thể loại (documentary) sẽ sụp đổ; 'the form' chỉ toàn bộ quy ước thể loại, không chỉ một tờ giấy
phần đó là lỗi của tôi; cách nhận trách nhiệm cụ thể, giới hạn rõ phạm vi mà không nhận lỗi toàn bộ

grammarNotes.title: Concessive 'but' and 'though' for nuanced argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Người dùng tiếng Anh ở mức C1 thường nhường nhịn một phần lập luận của đối phương trước khi đẩy luận điểm chính. Hai cấu trúc thường gặp: 'X — but/and yet Y' (thừa nhận X, vẫn giữ Y) và 'I'm not saying X. I'm saying Y' (loại trừ cách hiểu nhầm rồi tái khẳng định).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Watch how both speakers use this concession-then-counter rhythm. Aiyana: 'Editing is authorship. I won't pretend otherwise. But what you're describing… is doing exactly what documentary is supposed to do.' Marcus: 'I'm not accusing you of fabricating anything. I'm saying that selecting is itself a kind of writing.' Each concedes the opposing point before insisting on its own — a hallmark of C1-level argument.

translationVi:
Aiyana: Cảm ơn anh đã đến, Marcus. Tôi muốn nói chuyện trực tiếp.
Marcus: Tôi cảm kích. Nhưng quyết định của tôi thật sự không phải để bàn cãi. Tôi muốn cảnh ở quán ăn bị bỏ ra. Còn lại, chị giữ.
Aiyana: Tôi muốn tôn trọng yêu cầu đó. Nhưng cũng muốn hiểu nó. Hai năm trước, anh đã xem một bản dựng thô của cảnh ấy, ngay trong căn phòng này, và anh nói — đúng nguyên văn — 'giữ nó lại, đó là cốt lõi.' Điều gì đã thay đổi?
Marcus: Tôi đã thay đổi. Hay đúng hơn, tôi đã học được việc đứng phía bên kia của một chiếc máy quay không chớp mắt nghĩa là gì. Cảnh ấy đúng. Mỗi lời tôi nói, tôi đã nói. Nhưng cách sự im lặng đọng lại sau khi mẹ tôi bước ra kể một câu chuyện về tôi mà tôi không còn nghĩ là câu chuyện duy nhất.
Aiyana: Tôi tiếp nhận nghiêm túc. Nhưng tôi muốn phản biện một chỗ. Khung hình không tạo ra sự im lặng. Sự im lặng đã xảy ra. Việc của tôi là chĩa máy quay vào đó và không chớp mắt.
Marcus: Tôi không buộc tội chị bịa đặt. Tôi đang nói rằng việc chọn lựa, tự nó, đã là một dạng viết. Chị đã chọn tám giây ấy từ ba giờ đồng hồ — không nhạc, không cắt, không khoảng nghỉ. Đó không phải 'chĩa máy quay.' Đó là một câu chị đã viết, ngay vào mặt tôi.
Aiyana: Đúng. Dựng phim là một dạng tác quyền. Tôi sẽ không giả vờ ngược lại. Nhưng những lựa chọn đó đang làm chính xác điều mà phim tài liệu nên làm. Chúng từ chối làm dịu đi một thứ mà thế giới đưa cho ta một cách khắc nghiệt.
Marcus: Còn thế giới đang xem phim? Cháu trai tôi, sinh viên tôi, người đàn ông phục vụ chúng tôi ở quán — tất cả đã xem. Tôi không kiểm soát tám giây ấy có nghĩa gì với họ. Chị ngồi với đoạn phim suốt chín tháng và đi đến một ý nghĩa. Họ nhận nó trong tám giây và đi đến một bản án.
Aiyana: Tôi hiểu. Nhưng anh đã ký một bản đồng ý. Không chỉ là tờ giấy pháp lý — anh đã xem bản dựng và nói có. Nếu tôi để các nhân vật rút lại đồng ý hai năm sau khi phim ra, mọi bộ phim tài liệu tôi từng làm sẽ trở thành tạm bợ. Hình thức này sụp đổ.
Marcus: Tôi biết chị sẽ nói thế. Và tôi nghĩ chị đúng một phần. Nhưng hãy xem chị đang bảo vệ điều gì. Sự đồng ý tôi đưa là sự đồng ý của một người đàn ông chưa từng bị một người lạ trên tàu nhận ra và dùng tên mẹ anh ta như một câu đùa. Cái 'tôi' đã nói có không biết cái 'có' đó sẽ phải trả giá gì.
Aiyana: Đó là một điểm thật, và tôi sẽ không gạt đi. Nhưng nó có hai mặt. Cái 'tôi' đồng ý làm phim cũng không biết phim sẽ làm gì, và đã cam kết trước rằng sẽ đứng đằng sau nó. Nếu sự đồng ý chỉ tồn tại cho đến khi nó trở nên bất tiện, thì nó không phải đồng ý — nó là sở thích.
Marcus: Chị đang đối xử với yêu cầu của tôi như thể nó là một sự bốc đồng. Không phải. Tôi đang yêu cầu chị bỏ một cảnh, không phải dẹp cả phim. Khu phố, mẹ tôi, việc quán ăn đóng cửa — tất cả đều có thể giữ. Cái tôi yêu cầu là tám giây ấy, lấy ra từ phút tệ nhất đời tôi, không tiếp tục là khung hình tĩnh nằm trên poster liên hoan phim.
Aiyana: Đó là một yêu cầu khác, và công bằng hơn. Poster tôi có thể đổi. Trailer tôi có thể dựng lại. Còn việc tôi có nên gỡ cảnh ấy khỏi chính bộ phim hay không — tôi không biết.
Marcus: Phim dài 93 phút. Nó có thể là 92.
Aiyana: Nhưng cảnh ấy không phải đồ trang trí. Nó là bản lề. Nửa sau của phim được hiệu chỉnh dựa vào sự im lặng đó. Không có nó, kết phim mất đi cái ý nghĩa hiện tại. Anh gọi nó là 'cốt lõi' hai năm trước bởi vì đúng là vậy.
Marcus: Nó là cốt lõi của bộ phim chị muốn làm. Tôi không chắc nó là cốt lõi của đời tôi.
Aiyana: Đó là câu sạch nhất anh nói cả chiều nay. Hay là thế này: các bản chiếu hiện tại giữ nguyên. Với mọi phân phối tương lai — streaming, phát sóng — chúng ta đưa ra một bản cắt ngắn hơn, gỡ cảnh ấy, và có lời giới thiệu của anh ở đầu. Bản gốc nằm trong kho lưu trữ. Bản mà phần lớn người xem từ đây về sau là bản mà anh có thể đứng đằng sau.
Marcus: Một bản chỉnh của tác giả, nhưng từ nhân vật.
Aiyana: Một bản chỉnh của nhân vật. Thể loại này có lẽ cần sinh ra một thứ như thế.
Marcus: Để tôi nghĩ. Tôi đến đây sẵn sàng đánh nhau với chị, và thay vào đó chị đề nghị một thứ tôi không biết mình có thể đòi. Tuần sau tôi sẽ viết.
Aiyana: Hôm nay vậy là đủ. Dù anh quyết thế nào — cuộc trò chuyện chúng ta vừa có là cuộc trò chuyện đáng lẽ tôi phải có trước khi phim ra, không phải sau. Phần đó là lỗi của tôi.

questions.prompt: What specifically does Marcus want from Aiyana at the start of the conversation?
options: The removal of one particular sequence (the diner scene), with everything else kept.
The withdrawal of the entire film from circulation.
A formal apology and financial compensation.
Permission to make his own counter-film.
questions.explanation: Marcus: 'I'd like the sequence at the diner removed. Everything else, you can keep.'

questions.prompt: When Marcus says he has 'changed,' what specifically has changed about his view of the scene?
options: He no longer thinks the framing tells the only story about him that should be told.
He no longer remembers what he said in the scene.
He has decided the film is artistically poor.
He has been advised by a lawyer to retract consent.
questions.explanation: Marcus: 'it tells a story about me that I no longer think is the only story.'
questions.hint: Marcus's first long reply about being 'on the other side of a camera.'

questions.prompt: How does Aiyana initially defend the silence in the scene?
options: She says the frame did not invent the silence; the silence happened and her job was to point at it.
She says the silence was added by the sound editor.
She says other characters wanted the silence kept.
She denies that there is any silence in the scene.
questions.explanation: Aiyana: 'The frame did not invent the silence. The silence happened. My job was to point a camera at it and not flinch.'

questions.prompt: What is Marcus's core counter-argument to Aiyana's defence of 'just pointing a camera'?
options: That selecting eight seconds out of three hours is itself a kind of writing — i.e. authorship.
That the camera was placed at the wrong angle.
That the audio was manipulated in post-production.
That she should have used a different cinematographer.
questions.explanation: Marcus: 'selecting is itself a kind of writing. You chose those eight seconds out of three hours … That's a sentence you wrote, in my face.'
questions.hint: Marcus's reply after she says her job was to 'point a camera.'

questions.prompt: Does Aiyana eventually concede that editing is authorship?
options: Yes — she explicitly says 'Editing is authorship. I won't pretend otherwise.'
No — she insists throughout that selection is morally neutral.
She concedes only legally, not artistically.
She concedes only if Marcus signs a new release.
questions.explanation: She concedes directly, then argues that those authorial choices are doing what documentary is supposed to do.
questions.hint: Aiyana's turn after Marcus's 'sentence you wrote' line.

questions.prompt: What is Marcus's argument about audience reception (his nephew, his students, the man in the diner)?
options: Aiyana spent nine months arriving at a meaning; audiences get eight seconds and arrive at a verdict.
Audiences are too stupid to understand documentary film.
Documentary should never be shown publicly.
Audiences who pay for tickets deserve more footage.
questions.explanation: Marcus: 'You sat with the footage for nine months and arrived at a meaning. They get it in eight seconds and arrive at a verdict.'
questions.hint: Marcus's turn naming his nephew, students, and the man in the diner.

questions.prompt: What does Aiyana fear would happen if she allowed subjects to withdraw consent two years after release?
options: Every documentary she has ever made would become provisional, and the form would fall apart.
Other filmmakers would refuse to work with her.
questions.explanation: Aiyana: 'every documentary I have ever made becomes provisional. The form falls apart.'
questions.hint: Aiyana's first response invoking the legal release.

questions.prompt: What is Marcus's most philosophical objection to the consent argument?
options: That the 'me' who signed did not know what saying 'yes' would cost, because he had not yet experienced its consequences.
That he was not in a fit mental state when he signed.
That the release form was written in legal jargon he did not understand.
That the signature on the form was forged.
questions.explanation: Marcus: 'The 'me' who said yes did not know what yes would cost.'
questions.hint: Marcus's response to Aiyana's legal-release argument.

questions.prompt: How does Aiyana respond to Marcus's claim that the past 'me' did not know what consent would cost?
options: She agrees it's a real point but says it cuts both ways — her past self also committed in advance.
She offers to refund the appearance fee.
She accuses him of changing the subject.
questions.explanation: Aiyana: 'That is a real point, and I won't dismiss it. But it cuts both ways. The 'me' who agreed to make the film also did not know what the film would do.'
questions.hint: Aiyana's turn beginning with 'That is a real point.'

questions.prompt: What is the specific narrower request Marcus eventually clarifies?
options: That the eight-second still not be the image on the festival poster.
That the film be entirely re-edited from scratch.
That Aiyana stop showing the film publicly.
That his name be removed from the credits.
questions.explanation: Marcus: 'the eight seconds … not be the still that lives on the festival poster.'
questions.hint: Marcus's turn where he says 'I'm asking you to remove one scene, not to suppress the film.'

questions.prompt: Why does Aiyana resist simply removing the scene from the film itself?
options: She says it's 'the hinge' — the second half of the film is calibrated against that silence.
She says the scene is contractually required by the distributor.
She says it would be too expensive to re-edit.
She says she doesn't have access to the master files.
questions.explanation: Aiyana: 'But the scene isn't decoration. It's the hinge. Everything in the second half of the film is calibrated against that silence.'
questions.hint: Aiyana's turn after Marcus says the film could be 92 minutes instead of 93.

questions.prompt: What is the compromise Aiyana ultimately proposes?
options: The original stays archived; future distribution carries a shorter cut with the scene removed and Marcus's introduction at the beginning.
She will give Marcus full editorial control over the entire film.
She will pull the film from streaming for one year.
She will pay Marcus to keep silent about his objections.
questions.explanation: Aiyana proposes: existing prints unchanged, but 'for any future distribution … we offer a shorter cut, with the scene removed, and your introduction at the beginning.'
questions.hint: Aiyana's long turn near the end, after 'That's the cleanest sentence …'

questions.prompt: When Marcus says 'It was the whole point of the film you wanted to make. I'm not sure it was the whole point of my life,' what is he distinguishing?
options: An artistic centre (Aiyana's film) from a biographical centre (his life).
questions.explanation: He concedes the scene is artistically central to her film, while denying that it is the centre of his life — these are different centres of gravity.

questions.prompt: What does Aiyana mean when she calls the proposed shorter version 'a subject's edit'?
options: She is naming a new kind of edit, distinct from a director's or author's cut, originating from the subject of the documentary.
She means a draft made by a junior editor.
She means an edit covering only one subject of the curriculum.
She means an edit that removes the subject entirely.
questions.explanation: When Marcus says 'An author's edit, but from the subject,' she responds 'A subject's edit. I think the form might need to grow one' — coining the category.
questions.hint: Listen to the exchange between 'An author's edit' and 'A subject's edit.'

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That documentary subjects should automatically have the right to remove any scene at any time.
That editing involves authorial choices, not just neutral selection.
That a consent given two years ago may have unforeseen costs.
That a subject's view of a scene may legitimately change after release.
questions.explanation: Aiyana explicitly resists automatic, open-ended subject veto ('the form falls apart'). The dialogue ends in a negotiated compromise, not a default subject-edit right.

questions.prompt: Aiyana's last line — 'the conversation we just had is one I should have had with you before the film came out, not after. That part is on me.' — represents what kind of move?
options: A concession of her own ethical failure that is separate from whether the scene should be cut.
A refusal to take any responsibility.
A demand for an apology from Marcus.
An accusation that Marcus was uncooperative during filming.
questions.explanation: She isolates a separate failure (timing of the conversation) and accepts responsibility for it, without retracting her substantive position about the cut.
questions.hint: Notice what 'that part' refers to — only the conversation's timing, not the cut decision.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase best captures Marcus's worry about the 'eight seconds' versus 'nine months'?
options: Authors live with their authorial choices over time; audiences receive only the finished verdict — and the gap is morally significant.
Audiences spend longer in cinemas than directors spend editing.
Marcus thinks the scene is technically inaccurate.
Marcus thinks Aiyana spent too long in the editing room.
questions.explanation: His objection is precisely about the asymmetry between the maker's slow understanding and the audience's instant judgement.
questions.hint: Reread Marcus's lines about his nephew, students, and the man in the diner.

cloze.template:
Aiyana: Thank you for coming, Marcus. I wanted to have this in person.
Marcus: I appreciate that. But my decision is not really up for {{b1}}. I'd like the diner sequence removed. Everything else, you can keep.
Aiyana: I want to honour that. But also to understand it. Two years ago you watched a {{b2}} cut of that scene, in this same room, and said — your words — 'leave it in, that's the whole point.' What's changed?
Marcus: I've changed. Or rather, I've learned what it means to be on the other side of a camera that does not {{b3}}. The scene is accurate. Every word I said, I said. But the way the silence sits after my mother walks out tells a story about me that I no longer think is the only one.
Aiyana: I take that seriously. But I want to push back on one piece. The frame did not {{b4}} the silence. The silence happened. My job was to point a camera at it and not flinch.
Marcus: I'm not accusing you of fabricating anything. I'm saying {{b5}} is itself a kind of writing. You chose those eight seconds out of three hours — no music, no cut, no relief. That's not 'pointing a camera.' That's a sentence you wrote, in my face.
Aiyana: Fair. Editing is {{b6}}. I won't pretend otherwise. But those choices are doing exactly what documentary is supposed to do. They refuse to soften something the world hands us hard.
Marcus: And the world that watches the film? My nephew, my students, the man in the diner who served us — they've all seen it. I don't control what eight seconds mean to them. You sat with the footage for nine months and arrived at a meaning. They get it in eight seconds and arrive at a {{b7}}.
Aiyana: I understand. But you signed a release. Not just the legal form — you watched the cut and said yes. If I let subjects withdraw consent two years after release, every documentary I have ever made becomes {{b8}}. The form falls apart.
Marcus: I knew you'd say that. And I think you're partly right. But consider what you're defending. The consent I gave was the consent of a man who had not yet been recognised on a train by a stranger using my mother's first name as a {{b9}}. The 'me' who said yes did not know what yes would cost.
Aiyana: That is a real point, and I won't dismiss it. But it cuts both {{b10}}. The 'me' who agreed to make the film also did not know what it would do, and committed in advance to standing behind it. If consent only survives until it becomes inconvenient, it isn't consent — it's preference.
Marcus: You're treating my request as if it were a {{b11}}. It isn't. I'm asking you to remove one scene, not the film. The neighbourhood, my mother, the closing of the diner — all that can stay. What I'm asking is that the eight seconds, lifted from the worst minute of my life, not be the still that lives on the festival {{b12}}.
Aiyana: That's a different request, and fairer. The poster I can change. The trailer I can re-cut. Whether I should remove the scene from the film itself — I don't know.
Marcus: The film is ninety-three minutes. It can be ninety-two.
Aiyana: But the scene isn't decoration. It's the {{b13}}. The second half of the film is calibrated against that silence. Without it, the ending stops meaning what it means. You called it 'the whole point' two years ago because it was.
Marcus: It was the whole point of the film you wanted to make. I'm not sure it was the whole point of my {{b14}}.
Aiyana: That's the cleanest sentence you've said all afternoon. What if we did this: existing prints stay as they are. For future distribution — streaming, broadcast — we offer a shorter cut with the scene removed, and your {{b15}} at the beginning. The original stays in the archive. The version most people see going forward is the one you can stand behind.
Marcus: An {{b16}} edit, but from the subject.
Aiyana: A subject's edit. The form might need to grow one.
Marcus: Let me think. I came here ready to fight you, and instead you've offered something I didn't know I could ask for. I'll write next week.
Aiyana: That's enough for today. Whichever way you decide — the conversation we just had is one I should have had before the film came out, not after. That part is on {{b17}}.

blanks.explanation: Marcus announces his decision is not up for 'discussion' — i.e. he is not opening a negotiation, only stating a position.
A 'rough cut' is the standard industry term for an unfinished edit shown to subjects/producers.
The metaphor is that the camera 'does not blink' — it does not look away.
Aiyana's defence: the frame did not 'invent' the silence — meaning the silence existed in reality.
Marcus's claim: it is the act of 'selecting' (eight seconds from three hours) that constitutes authorship.
Aiyana concedes: 'Editing is authorship.' She does not retreat to 'neutrality.'
The contrast is between her nine months of 'meaning' and the audience's eight-second 'verdict' — a judgement.
Provisional = subject to being withdrawn. Allowing late retraction would make every prior film 'provisional.'
Marcus reports that a stranger used his mother's first name 'as a punchline' — a moment of public mockery.
Idiomatic: 'cuts both ways' = the argument applies equally to both sides.
Marcus accuses her of treating his request as a 'whim' — i.e. a momentary impulse — when it isn't.
The specific image he objects to is the still on the festival 'poster.'
Aiyana's metaphor: the scene is 'the hinge' on which the film's second half turns.
His pivotal line: the scene was the whole point of the film, but not of his 'life.'
Her proposed compromise includes a recorded 'introduction' from Marcus at the start of the shorter cut.
Marcus names the proposed format by analogy to the 'author's edit' (or author's cut) — but originating from the subject.
Aiyana accepts responsibility for the conversation's timing: 'That part is on me.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Marcus says that the 'me' who signed the release did not know what saying yes would cost. Aiyana counters that the 'me' who agreed to make the film also did not know what the film would do. Both are true. Where should the burden of an uncertain future fall — on the person whose face is in the frame, or on the person who chose the frame? Does your answer change if the subject is a child, an elected official, or a private citizen in a moment of grief?
